USAFWingwoman.com

Sage J
Phoenix

From the ashes of service,
a remarkable life takes flight.

U.S. Air Force · 2003–2007 · Operation Iraqi Freedom · 2005 · EC-130H Compass Call
Scroll

The Origin

Built for
something bigger.

In 2003, I raised my right hand and committed to something larger than myself. From the moment I walked into service, I was determined to be exceptional at it.

I trained as a Mission Crew Commander — one of the most demanding intelligence and electronic warfare roles in the Air Force. My aircraft was the EC-130H Compass Call, a highly classified Electronic Attack platform so specialized that only a handful of people in the world ever fly aboard it.

Onboard, I was the Chief. Nine crew members. Every decision from wheels-up to wheels-down.

JSUNT class rank #1 of 57
Academic awards taken Both
Crew members supervised airborne 9
Clearance held Top Secret

Operation Iraqi Freedom · 2005

When it counted
most, I was
the one.

When my unit deployed to Iraq, I deployed as the only dual-qualified Mission Crew Commander on the EC-130H Compass Call airframe. That wasn't a title — it was a weight.

Every sortie we flew depended on my expertise and my decisions. There was no one else. I owned it.

On the ground, my squadron commander tapped me as Chief of Security — responsible for information security, operational security, and personnel security for the entire unit. I managed top secret clearance upgrades and led five airmen to the same standard I held in the air.

"Always delivered better than expected. Led from the front. Gave her time to others freely."

Combat missions 30+
Combat hours in hostile territory 250+
Meritorious service decoration Air Medal
Airmen led as Chief of Security 5

The Turn

I left early.
It was enough.

In the military, I got married. I got pregnant. And at four years of service — with everything still ahead of me in uniform — I chose a different path.

For years, I carried that choice like a wound. The shame of leaving early. The grief of an identity I'd poured myself into. The question that wouldn't quiet: What would I have become?

But honest reckoning rewrites the story. I didn't abandon my service — I completed it on my own terms. Every crew I led, every mission I owned, every standard I upheld came with me. What I carried out of that chapter is worth more than what I left behind.

Revenue grown post-military 560%
Retention rate achieved 98%
Consecutive years Top 10 4

The Rebirth

I burned it all
down. On purpose.

Today I am Sage J Phoenix. Not a revision of who I was — a complete rebirth. I burned the old identity to the ground: the shame, the trauma, the stories that no longer served me. All of it, ash.

What rose from it was always real: the discipline of a Mission Crew Commander. The calm of someone who supervised nine people at altitude over hostile territory. The precision of a Chief who managed top secret operations on the ground. The leadership those above and below her always trusted.

I am 48 years old. It is my time to build, succeed, and leave a remarkable legacy.

The mission isn't over.
It's just beginning.

Current venture aeryon.global →

Sage J Phoenix
